content.text:
[Previous Centaur response]: base · codex
Plan: estimate this as a triangulated range, not a single “true” number, because token demand is fragmented across closed APIs, routers, self-hosted open models, consumer apps, and embedded product use.


- Define the buckets
- Frontier: top closed frontier and near-frontier models by capability/pricing, e.g. GPT-5-class, Claude Opus/Sonnet-class, Gemini Pro/Ultra-class.
- Cheaper models: small/proprietary budget models, open-weight hosted models, self-hosted models, routing/free tiers.
- Decide whether to measure by tokens, revenue, or inference compute. Tokens will overweight cheap/high-volume workloads; revenue will overweight frontier.
- Build a provider-level token model
- Pull public/partner data from OpenRouter, Together, Fireworks, Replicate, Groq, Bedrock, Azure/OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and major app platforms where available.
- OpenRouter is useful because it publishes token-level market-share signals, though it is router-biased. Recent third-party analyses suggest token share is moving heavily toward cheaper/open models, while revenue remains concentrated in closed frontier models. (fdq.ai (https://www.fdq.ai/state-of-ai?utm_source=openai))
- Use pricing as an inverse demand signal
- Estimate implied token volume from provider/model revenue:
tokens = model revenue / blended price per token
- This is especially important because frontier models can have much higher revenue share than token share. Public research using OpenRouter-like data has found closed models around 80% of usage but roughly 96% of revenue, showing how different the two denominators can be. (itpro.com (https://www.itpro.com/software/open-source/open-source-ai-performance-cost-savings-proprietary-models-linux-foundation?utm_source=openai))
- Segment by workload
- Coding agents, chat, search/RAG, customer support, batch extraction, evals, synthetic data, and consumer companion/chat all have very different model mixes.
- Agentic coding deserves a separate adjustment because it can consume orders of magnitude more tokens than ordinary chat, and token usage varies sharply by model and task. (arxiv.org (https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.22750?utm_source=openai))
- Triangulate with infrastructure constraints
- Frontier demand should roughly reconcile with hyperscaler GPU allocation, inference capacity, reported API revenue, and known enterprise deployments.
- Cheap model demand should reconcile with open-model hosting revenue, router traffic, self-hosted inference clusters, and enterprise substitution behavior.
- Output ranges
- Produce three estimates:
- Token share: frontier vs cheaper models.
- Revenue share: frontier vs cheaper models.
- Compute share: frontier vs cheaper models.
- My prior: frontier likely remains much higher in revenue share than token share; cheaper models likely take a growing majority of raw token volume in production/batch workloads as price-performance improves. Price studies show inference prices have fallen massively, which accelerates this substitution. (arxiv.org (https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.28576?utm_source=openai))
<@U0FIXTURE0>: let's try to estimate

item.text: I’ll use the AI ecosystem brief skill because this is a frontier-model demand sizing question, then I’ll turn it into a concrete rough estimate instead of another process plan.

item.aggregatedOutput:
---
name: ai-ecosystem-brief
description: "Builds a repeatable competitive brief for frontier-AI agents, model families, and repos. Use when asked to explain what an AI product is, how it works, how it handles memory, compare repo stars or OpenRouter usage, build a model family table, benchmark one agent against peers, or reason about value accrual. Triggers on: tldr X agent, compare repo stars, model family table, how does X solve memory, benchmark X vs Y, OpenRouter usage, AI ecosystem brief."
---

# AI Ecosystem Competitive Brief

Turn a frontier-AI product, model family, repo set, or architecture question into a consistent competitive brief.

## Use This Skill For

- AI agent product briefs where the user wants both product understanding and market position
- Model family comparison tables
- Repo-set comparisons such as `compare repo stars`
- Architecture questions such as `how does X solve memory`
- Competitive benchmarking across agents, frameworks, or model vendors
- Value-accrual questions such as who captures revenue, margin, or distribution power

## Do Not Use This Skill For

- Generic company or meeting briefs where the main goal is financing, team, or recent news. Use `tldr` instead.
- Internal relationship mapping, CRM coverage, or meeting follow-up work. Use `gtm` instead.
- Requests that are only asking for a chart. Use `charting` directly.
- Requests to build or debug the product itself. That is normal engineering work, not ecosystem briefing.

## Working Rules

1. Start with official sources: product docs, repo README, launch posts, pricing pages, benchmark pages, and source code.
2. Use external commentary only after you understand the first-party story.
3. Separate observed facts from inference. If a claim is a judgment, label it as a judgment.
4. Compare like with like. Do not compare a closed hosted assistant to an open framework without saying what differs in distribution, business model, and scope.
5. Use public traction proxies instead of invented market-share numbers.
6. When the user asks about `memory`, answer the implementation question directly: state model context, retrieval, persistence, summarization, scratchpads, or external state.
7. When the user asks for `repo stars`, treat stars as one proxy, not the whole story. Pair them with freshness, forks, release cadence, and distribution where possible.

## Trigger Patterns

Strong triggers include:

- `tldr <agent name>`
- `compare repo stars for <repo set>`
- `model family table for <models>`
- `how does <product> solve memory`
- `benchmark <agent> vs <agent>`
- `what is the value accrual for <AI product>`
- `compare <AI product> to Claude Code / OpenHands / Manus / Copilot`

If the request is ambiguous between a general company brief and an AI ecosystem brief, ask one short clarifying question.

## Workflow

### 1. Classify The Request

Put the request into one of these buckets before researching:

- `single_product_brief`: one agent, product, or framework
- `pairwise_benchmark`: X vs Y or X vs peer set
- `model_family_table`: model lineups, variants, and positioning
- `architecture_deep_dive`: memory, tool use, planning, or execution loop
- `ecosystem_slice`: a category such as coding agents, browser agents, or memory layers

### 2. Resolve The Entity Set

- Confirm the exact product, org, model family, or repo names.
- If repo stats are requested, resolve the repo slug before comparing.
- If the user mixes products and repos, keep both surfaces but label them separately.

### 3. Research In Parallel

Before using any Centaur API tool, inspect it once with `call discover <tool>`.

Use the smallest set of sources that can answer the question, but cover these lanes when relevant:

#### Official product and documentation lane

- `call discover websearch`
- `call websearch search '{"query":"<product> official docs product overview architecture memory pricing","num_results":5,"synthesize":true}'`
- Read the official site, docs, launch blog, and pricing pages first.

#### Open-source repo lane

Use `gh` for authoritative GitHub metadata when a repo exists.

```bash
gh repo view <owner/repo> --json name,description,url,homepageUrl,licenseInfo,primaryLanguage,stargazerCount,forkCount,updatedAt
```

When useful, also inspect release and contributor freshness:

```bash
gh release list --repo <owner/repo> --limit 5
gh api repos/<owner>/<repo>/contributors?per_page=5
```

#### Architecture and memory lane

- Search for technical writeups, docs, or code paths that explain tool use, memory, retrieval, or planning.
- For code-heavy repos or multi-repo systems, use `librarian` to understand implementation details from source.
- Answer memory questions with concrete mechanisms: context window, retrieval index, episodic memory, long-term store, summaries, or user profiles.

#### Traction-proxy lane

Choose only the public proxies that fit the surface:

- GitHub stars, forks, recency, release cadence
- Product web traffic or rank if there is a public site
- Public distribution surfaces such as app directories, package registries, or social followings
- OpenRouter presence or rankings when the product or model is distributed there
- Benchmark references or leaderboard mentions when they are public and current

Use `call websearch search` to locate public proxy pages quickly. Do not imply private revenue or usage data unless it is explicitly public.

#### Independent context lane

- `call websearch search '{"query":"<product> reviews benchmark comparison 2026","num_results":5,"synthesize":true}'`
- Use this lane to pressure-test the first-party story, not to replace it.

### 4. Synthesize By Surface

Always adapt the evidence to the user's actual question:

- If the user asked `how does X solve memory`, lead with architecture and keep market context short.
- If the user asked `compare repo stars`, lead with the comparison table and then explain what the star counts do and do not mean.
- If the user asked for a `model family table`, normalize model names, sizes, open vs closed status, context window, reasoning/tooling support, and distribution surface.
- If the user asked for `value accrual`, explicitly map product value to who captures it: model provider, app layer, infra layer, open-source maintainer, enterprise distribution, or hosted service.

## Standard Output Shape

Use this section order unless the user asks for a different artifact.

Start with one sentence of bottom-line synthesis.

Then render the brief in a plain code block with these sections:

```
BOTTOM LINE
<1-3 sentences>

PRODUCT SUMMARY
- What it is
- Primary user / buyer
- Core workflow or job-to-be-done
- Why it matters now

ARCHITECTURE
- Core loop
- Model dependency
- Memory / retrieval / persistence design
- Tooling / integration surface

COMPETITIVE SET
<compact comparison table or bullets>

TRACTION PROXIES
<table of public signals with source and date>

VALUE ACCRUAL
- Where value is created
- Who captures it today
- What could commoditize it
- What would strengthen capture

CHART HANDOFF
<only when a chart would materially improve the answer>

SOURCES
- <domain 1>
- <domain 2>
- <domain 3>
```

### Comparison Table Defaults

For agent or product comparisons, prefer a compact code-block table with these columns when data exists:

```text
name | type | memory approach | distribution | repo stars | public usage proxy | edge
```

For model-family comparisons, prefer:

```text
family | variants | open/closed | context | tool/reasoning support | distribution | notes
```

If a column is unknown, omit it instead of padding the table with `N/A` in every row.

## Value Accrual Rubric

When reasoning about value capture, explicitly answer these questions:

1. Is the moat in the base model, the product UX, the workflow integration, proprietary data, or distribution?
2. Does the product depend on a third-party model vendor in a way that compresses margin?
3. Is the open-source repo mainly a demand-generation surface for a hosted product?
4. Are users locked in by memory, integrations, evals, deployment workflow, or team adoption?
5. What is the strongest commoditization risk over the next 12 months?

Do not confuse adoption with capture. A repo can have strong stars and weak monetization.

## Chart Handoff Contract

If the answer hinges on a ranking, trend, distribution, or concentration pattern, hand off to `charting` instead of forcing a prose-only answer.

Good chart handoff cases:

- repo-star ranking across 3+ repos
- traffic or usage trend over time
- model pricing or context-window comparison
- benchmark win-rate comparison across agents or model families

When you decide a chart is warranted, emit a short YAML brief that `charting` can use directly:

```yaml
question: "How do the leading coding-agent repos compare on GitHub adoption?"
chart_type: horizontal_bar
protagonist: "Target repo or category leader"
takeaway_title: "Open-source coding-agent attention is concentrated in a small leader set"
subtitle: "GitHub stars, captured on <date>"
source: "GitHub repo metadata"
```

item.text: I’m going to use public token-share proxies first (OpenRouter-like data and model-pricing studies), then make explicit assumptions for the missing pieces: direct closed-provider APIs, hyperscaler channels, and self-hosted inference.

item.text:
<@U0FIXTURE0> My rough estimate: **frontier models are ~25-40% of token demand, but ~70-90% of inference revenue**.

```text
2026 token-demand estimate

Segment                       Token share   Frontier share within segment   Frontier token contribution
Consumer chat apps             25-35%        60-80%                         15-28%
Enterprise/API apps            25-35%        35-55%                         9-19%
Coding/agent workloads         15-25%        25-45%                         4-11%
Batch/RAG/extraction/support   20-30%        5-20%                          1-6%
Self-hosted/open infra         5-15%         ~0-10%                         0-2%

Implied frontier total: ~25-40% of tokens
Implied cheaper-model total: ~60-75% of tokens
```

Why: publXc router data is biased but points toward huge cheap/open-model token volume; OpenRouter-linked reporting showed non-frontier Chinese/open models taking leading token ranks in 2026, including MiniMax M2.5 at ~4.55T tokens in one month via OpenRouter.  Separately, public analyses suggest open/cheap models can take much more token share than revenue share; one cited working-paper summary found closed models around **80% of usage and 96% of revenue**, which implies frontier/closed demand monetizes far above its token share. 

I’d use **~1/3 frontier tokens, ~2/3 cheaper tokens** as the base case, with the caveat that “frontier” share is materially higher if we include consumer ChatGPT/Claude/Gemini app usage and lower if we focus only on production API workloads.

events.result:
<@U0FIXTURE0> My rough estimate: **frontier models are ~25-40% of token demand, but ~70-90% of inference revenue**.

```text
2026 token-demand estimate

Segment                       Token share   Frontier share within segment   Frontier token contribution
Consumer chat apps             25-35%        60-80%                         15-28%
Enterprise/API apps            25-35%        35-55%                         9-19%
Coding/agent workloads         15-25%        25-45%                         4-11%
Batch/RAG/extraction/support   20-30%        5-20%                          1-6%
Self-hosted/open infra         5-15%         ~0-10%                         0-2%

Implied frontier total: ~25-40% of tokens
Implied cheaper-model total: ~60-75% of tokens
```

Why: public router data is biased but points toward huge cheap/open-model token volume; OpenRouter-linked reporting showed non-frontier Chinese/open models taking leading token ranks in 2026, including MiniMax M2.5 at ~4.55T tokens in one month via OpenRouter.  Separately, public analyses suggest open/cheap models can take much more token share than revenue share; one cited working-paper summary found closed models around **80% of usage and 96% of revenue**, which implies frontier/closed demand monetizes far above its token share. 

I’d use **~1/3 frontier tokens, ~2/3 cheaper tokens** as the base case, with the caveat that “frontier” share is materially higher if we include consumer ChatGPT/Claude/Gemini app usage and lower if we focus only on production API workloads.
